one_line_takeaway: Tr-GoF is a truncated goodness-of-fit watermark detector that provably and adaptively reaches the optimal robust-detection boundary (q+2p=1) under human/adversarial edits, where prior sum-based detectors are stuck at a strictly weaker q+p=1/2.
key_finding: Sum-based watermark detectors (the dominant prior approach) are fundamentally suboptimal against edits -- their additive statistics are corrupted by edit-induced noise, capping them at detection boundary q+p=1/2, whereas Tr-GoF reaches the information-theoretic optimum q+2p=1 with no tuning or knowledge of the edit level. Empirically the gain is largest exactly where it matters (adversarial edits, low temperature / deterministic outputs): the motivating experiment shows that with only 5% of tokens edited and length 400, prior methods' power falls from 87.8% (edit-free) to 64.7% (paraphrase) to 30.2% (adversarial), and Tr-GoF substantially closes this gap. Surprisingly, a method designed for the SPARSE (heavy-edit) regime also turns out optimal in the DENSE (constant-edit) regime.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Tr-GoF (Truncated Goodness-of-Fit), a statistical test for detecting LLM watermarks in text that has been partially altered by human (or adversarial) edits. Its own contributions, distinct from the cited framework of Li et al. [47] on which it builds, are: (1) a mixture-model formulation of human edits, where each observed token's pivotal statistic Y_t is drawn from (1-eps_n)*mu_0 + eps_n*mu_{1,P_t} because an edited token's recomputed pseudorandomness becomes statistically independent of the token (broken watermark = noise); (2) a phase-transition detectability theory in the (p,q) plane (eps_n ~ n^-p sparsity of surviving signal, Delta_n ~ n^-q NTP singularity) proving the optimal detection boundary is q+2p=1, and proving that ALL monotone parameter-free sum-based rules (as used by prior methods) are strictly suboptimal at only q+p=1/2; (3) the Tr-GoF test itself, a phi_s-divergence GoF statistic with two novel truncations (on the r-domain to drop extreme small p-values, and truncating K_s to K_s^+) that achieves the optimal q+2p=1 boundary ADAPTIVELY with no knowledge of edit level eps, NTP regularity Delta, or P_t; (4) a proof that Tr-GoF also attains the optimal class-dependent P_Delta-efficiency rate in the dense/constant-edit regime, matching the KL-optimal rate D_KL(mu_0, (1-eps)mu_0 + eps*mu_{1,P*_Delta}). Higher Criticism is shown to be a special case (s=2).
threat_model: Three-party watermarking protocol: a model provider embeds an unbiased Gumbel-max watermark using a secret key; a user edits the generated text before use; a verifier holding the key/hash tries to decide whether the (possibly edited) text is machine-generated. The verifier observes ONLY the final edited text -- not the prompt, not the original unedited generation, not the internal NTP distributions. The adversary/user weakens the watermark via edits: random substitution/insertion/deletion, paraphrase (WordNet synonym) edits, roundtrip translation (EN->FR->EN), and worst-case 'adversarial' edits that selectively remove tokens carrying the strongest watermark signal within an edit budget. Any edit inside the m-token context window (m=5 in experiments) breaks the pseudorandomness for that position, turning signal into noise; the verifier cannot tell which tokens were edited. The security goal is a detection rule that stays powerful and, crucially, ADAPTIVE -- effective without prior knowledge of how much or how the text was edited.
limitations: Scope is the Gumbel-max (Aaronson/OpenAI) unbiased watermark specifically; generality to green-red-list or other schemes is discussed only qualitatively. Optimality is asymptotic (n -> infinity) under stylized assumptions -- Assumption 4.1 treats pseudorandomness as perfectly i.i.d./independent of the past, and Assumption 4.2 assumes a uniform singularity Delta_n ~ n^-q across all NTP distributions (relaxed only slightly in Remark 4.2). Detection is provably IMPOSSIBLE in the heavy-edit regime (p>1/2), a hard ceiling no method escapes. In real LLM experiments Delta-regularity cannot be controlled and is only proxied by temperature. Tr-GoF's advantage is concentrated at low temperature; at temperature 0.7 it slightly trails hars (mean Type II gap ~0.02). It also assumes an honest verifier who holds the secret key and hash and can reconstruct the pseudorandom sequence.

datasets_used: C4 news-like dataset (1,000 documents as prompts; 5,000 unwatermarked C4 texts used to measure Type I error)
100 popular poems (poem-recitation and poem-generation tasks for edit-tolerance limits)
Synthetic pivotal-statistic samples (vocab |W|=5 and |W|=1000, text length n up to 10^4, N=10^3 trials, NTP generators M1 Zipf-law and M2 point-mass)
quantitative_results: Motivating degradation (Gumbel-max, length 400, 5% tokens edited, alpha=0.01): detection power 87.8% edit-free -> 64.7% under paraphrase edits -> 30.2% under adversarial edits
Optimal robust detection boundary proven at q+2p=1 (Tr-GoF, Theorems 4.2/4.3); sum-based rules limited to q+p=1/2 (Theorem 4.4 / Corollary 4.1)
Heavy-edit impossibility: for p in (1/2,1], Type I + Type II error -> 1 for any test (Theorem 4.1)
Empirical Type I error stays within [0.006, 0.014] across all methods at alpha=0.01 (5,000 C4 unwatermarked texts, OPT-1.3B)
Type II error at temperature 0.1 with 400 watermarked tokens: baselines ~0.6 vs best Tr-GoF ~0.3
At temperature 0.5, most methods reach Type II error 0.01 with only ~100 tokens; at temperature 0.7, ~50 tokens
Edit tolerance limits, Poem Recitation, OPT-1.3B (Tr-GoF s=2, bold-highest vs hars): Substitution 39.49% vs 37.96%; Insertion 45.64% vs 44.12%; Deletion 42.67% vs 41.92%
Tr-GoF s=2 consistently achieves the highest edit-tolerance across all three edit types and both poem tasks; at temp 0.7 it trails hars by only ~0.02 mean Type II error
Optimal dense-regime efficiency: sup over measurable S of R_{P_Delta}(S) = D_KL(mu_0, (1-eps)mu_0 + eps*mu_{1,P*_Delta}) = R_{P_Delta}(Tr-GoF) (Theorem 4.5)
Higher Criticism recovered exactly as Tr-GoF special case: n*S_n^+(2) = (1/2)(HC_n^+)^2
baselines_compared: Optimal least-favorable sum-based score h_opt,Delta from Li et al. [47] (Delta in {0.1,0.2,0.3}; Delta=0.1 best, also 0.2/0.3 in edit-tolerance table)
Indicator score h_ind,0.5 (in simulation boundary study)
Neyman-Pearson likelihood-ratio test (optimal but impractical theoretical benchmark)
